note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's break tags and sentence/comma boundaries into caption-sized pieces. Block windows are the blocks' MEASURED positions in the joined master: the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 288.576s but the ffprobed master is 288.131s (0.446s over, past the ±0.3s gate) because each ffprobe per-block duration carries a little mp3 encoder/decoder padding that does not accumulate in the concatenation. Onsets were measured by FFT normalized cross-correlation of each sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM); every block embeds at ncc≈1.000, consecutive onset gaps are all exactly 0.400s, and the trailing gap is 0.400s. Final end = ffprobed master duration 288.131s.

phrases.text: Start in Settings, and open the Hardware group.
Five lists live here, and together they describe the machines at a wash:
the terminals that exist, the screen layouts they run, and the lube-bay buttons they show.
Think of this as the field tech's corner of Settings,
the place you check before you touch anything at the site.
Click Hardware / Terminals.
Every machine a wash runs shows up here as one row:
its location, its address on the network, the build it runs, and when it last checked in.
When a site reports a dead register, start here.
A terminal that isn't listed, or whose last-seen time is hours stale,
has already told you something.
The rows didn't load today, so read the columns.
Now Screen Layout.
This one loaded.
The tiles count six layouts, split evenly between POS and kiosk,
and each row pins a screen type and a resolution to a location.
A portrait kiosk, a landscape register, each with its own entry.
One quirk: the card claims six records but lists two.
Trust the tiles for the real count, and treat the rows as samples.
System Types is next.
It's a short lookup list, one description per row,
the kind of behind-the-scenes list other pages point at.
Nothing to configure day to day.
Today it didn't load either, so note what it is and move on.
When you see "failed to fetch" on a page like this, it's the data service, not an empty list.
Lube Buttons.
Here's button wiring at its plainest.
Twenty-four buttons across a few lube-bay terminals, one row each.
The row names the group it belongs to, the terminal it appears on,
the label the operator taps, and the tile's color.
Oil Change in teal, Tire Rotation in blue.
When someone says a lube button is wrong, this is the row you read.
Lube Checklists.
One column, the service.
These are the checklist items a lube bay works through on a job.
Like System Types, it didn't load today,
so read the column and the purpose, not the values.
A menu tour like this is about knowing where each list lives,
so that when a ticket points here you go straight to it.
Collapse Hardware, and open UI and Display.
Eleven lists this time.
Most are the paint and the plumbing: button types, images, colors, templates.
We'll read the ones a technician gets asked about:
the cashier and greeter buttons, the button groups behind them,
the macros, and the kiosk screen flows.
The rest are the lookups those five draw from.
Button Groups.
A group is just the named set a button is filed under,
the same group value you saw on the lube buttons a moment ago.
Two columns, a description and whether it's active.
It didn't load today, so read the shape.
Groups are the folder; the buttons are what's inside.
Cashier Buttons.
These are the tap targets on the register screen.
Each row is a button: its slot number, its label,
the kind of action it runs, and its tile color.
So "the void button moved" or "the wrong color" is answered right here, one row per button.
The list didn't load today, but the columns tell the story.
Greeter Buttons.
Same four columns, aimed at the greeter station out at the lane instead of the register.
Keeping the two lists apart lets a site put different buttons
in front of the greeter than the cashier.
If a lane's entry screen looks wrong, you check here, not the cashier page.
This one didn't load today either.
Macros.
A macro is a saved action a button or a scanned barcode can fire.
The columns tell you its type, whether a barcode triggers it,
and whether it also shows on the handheld.
That last one matters when an operator says a shortcut works
at the register but not on the handheld device.
It didn't load today, so read the columns.
Last list: Kiosk Screen Flows.
This is where self-serve kiosk screens are built and pushed live.
The tiles count three flows, two published and one draft, across eleven screens.
Each row is a flow: its locations, how many screens and tappable hotspots, and a status chip.
Read the chip first.
Published is live on the kiosks;
a draft, like Express Tunnel, is built but not out yet.
The create button here reads New Flow.
So that's Hardware and UI and Display.
Hardware registers the machines and their screens.
UI and Display wires the buttons a cashier and greeter tap,
the macros behind them, and the kiosk flows a site publishes.
Two groups, ten lists, all read-only for you.
Learn where each one lives, because a POS ticket usually ends on one of these pages.
